1:
"My brethren, be not many masters, knowing that we shall receive the greater condemnation." The witnesses note the warning against many becoming teachers. On "be not many masters," the grammatical voices note the caution. Barnes and Robertson note the meaning of "masters": "be not many masters (didaskaloi — teachers); not many of you should become teachers" — James warns against too many eagerly taking up the role of teacher in the church, presuming to instruct others.

On "be not many masters," the witnesses note the presumption. Barnes and Calvin note that the warning is against the ambition to teach — many were eager to be teachers, to assume authority and display knowledge, without the gravity the office requires; the desire to instruct others should be checked.

On "knowing that we shall receive the greater condemnation," the puritan Henry notes the heavier accountability: teachers "shall receive the greater condemnation (krima — judgment)" — those who teach are judged more strictly, held to a higher standard, answerable for their words and example; the greater responsibility brings greater accountability. The verse displays the warning against many becoming teachers.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the caution — "be not many masters (teachers)," not many to become teachers, against eagerly taking up the role (Barnes, Robertson); the warning against the ambition to teach, the desire to instruct checked (Barnes, Calvin); "the greater condemnation (judgment)," teachers judged more strictly, the greater responsibility bringing greater accountability (Henry). The verse displays the warning. The verse marks the masters: not many. The verse displays the role: teaching. The verse displays the presumption: checked. The verse displays the accountability: greater condemnation. The caution displays the teaching office under stricter judgment. The verse displays the teachers: few. The verse displays the judgment: greater. The verse displays the responsibility: heavy. The warning against many becoming teachers displays the opening of the chapter on the tongue: not many should become teachers, knowing that those who teach receive the greater judgment — held to a stricter account for their words. The verse displays the stricter judgment, grounded in the universal stumbling of the tongue (v.2).
2:
"For in many things we offend all. If any man offend not in word, the same is a perfect man, and able also to bridle the whole body." The witnesses note that the one who controls his tongue is a perfect man. On "in many things we offend all," the grammatical voices note universal failure. Barnes and Robertson note the humbling admission: "in many things we offend (stumble, fail) all (all of us)" — everyone, teachers included, stumbles in many ways; universal human frailty, which is reason for caution in teaching.

On "if any man offend not in word, the same is a perfect man," the witnesses note the mark of maturity. Barnes and Calvin note the test: "if any man offend not in word (does not stumble in speech), the same is a perfect (teleios — mature, complete) man" — the one who never errs in speech is a fully mature man; control of the tongue is the surest mark of completeness, for the tongue is the hardest member to govern.

On "able also to bridle the whole body," the grammatical-historical note the inference: such a man is "able also to bridle (rein in, control) the whole body" — if he can control the tongue (the most unruly member), he can control his whole self; mastery of the tongue implies mastery of all. The verse displays that the one who controls his tongue is a perfect man.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: universal failure — "in many things we offend (stumble) all," everyone stumbling in many ways, reason for caution in teaching (Barnes, Robertson); "if any man offend not in word, the same is a perfect (mature) man," control of speech the mark of maturity, the tongue hardest to govern (Barnes, Calvin); "able also to bridle the whole body," mastery of the tongue implying mastery of all (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the perfect man. The verse marks the stumbling: all. The verse displays the test: offend not in word. The verse displays the maturity: a perfect man. The verse displays the inference: bridle the whole body. The teaching displays tongue-control as the mark of complete self-mastery. The verse displays the offending: universal. The verse displays the word: controlled. The verse displays the body: bridled. That the one who controls his tongue is a perfect man displays the measure of maturity: since all stumble in many ways, the man who never errs in speech is fully mature — for if he can bridle the tongue, the hardest member, he can control his whole self. The verse displays the bridled body, illustrated by the horse's bit (v.3).
3:
"Behold, we put bits in the horses' mouths, that they may obey us; and we turn about their whole body." The brief verse notes the figure of the bit that turns the horse. The grammatical voices note the first illustration. Barnes and Robertson note the comparison of the tongue to a bit: "we put bits (the bridle-bit) in the horses' mouths, that they may obey us; and we turn about (guide, steer) their whole body" — a small bit in the horse's mouth controls and directs the whole animal; a tiny instrument governs a great body.

On "that they may obey us," the witnesses note the control. Barnes and Calvin note that the bit makes the horse obey — by this small means the rider governs the powerful animal; the point is the disproportion between the small instrument and the great effect.

On "we turn about their whole body," the grammatical-historical note the application: as the small bit turns the whole horse, so the tongue, though small, governs and directs the whole man; the figure prepares the argument that a little member has great power (v.5). The verse displays the figure of the bit that turns the horse.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the illustration — "we put bits in the horses' mouths, that they may obey us; and we turn about (guide) their whole body," the small bit controlling the whole horse (Barnes, Robertson); the bit making the horse obey, a small means governing a powerful animal (Barnes, Calvin); the small bit turning the whole horse, as the tongue governs the whole man (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the bit. The verse marks the bit: in the mouth. The verse displays the obedience: of the horse. The verse displays the turning: the whole body. The verse displays the application: the tongue's power. The figure displays a small instrument governing a great body. The verse displays the bits: small. The verse displays the horse: controlled. The verse displays the body: turned. The figure of the bit that turns the horse displays the first illustration of the tongue's power: a small bit in the horse's mouth governs and steers the whole animal — so a little member like the tongue directs the whole man. The verse displays the governing bit, paralleled by the ship's small helm (v.4).
4:
"Behold also the ships, which though they be so great, and are driven of fierce winds, yet are they turned about with a very small helm, whithersoever the governor listeth." The brief verse notes the figure of the small helm steering the great ship. The grammatical voices note the second illustration. Barnes and Robertson note the comparison: "the ships, which though they be so great, and are driven of fierce winds, yet are they turned about (steered) with a very small helm (rudder), whithersoever the governor (pilot, steersman) listeth (wills, desires)" — a huge ship, driven by strong winds, is steered by a tiny rudder according to the pilot's will.

On "so great... driven of fierce winds," the witnesses note the great force. Barnes and Ellicott note the contrast: the ship is huge and driven by powerful winds, yet a small rudder controls it; the greater the disproportion, the more striking the point — a small thing governs a great and forceful one.

On "a very small helm," the grammatical-historical note the application: as the small rudder steers the great ship, so the small tongue directs the whole course of a man's life; the two figures (bit and helm) together establish that a little member wields great power. The verse displays the figure of the small helm steering the great ship.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the illustration — "the ships, though so great, and driven of fierce winds, yet are turned about with a very small helm (rudder), whithersoever the governor (pilot) listeth," a huge wind-driven ship steered by a tiny rudder (Barnes, Robertson); the great ship and powerful winds yet controlled by a small rudder, the striking disproportion (Barnes, Ellicott); the small rudder steering the great ship as the tongue directs the man (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the helm. The verse marks the ships: great. The verse displays the winds: fierce. The verse displays the helm: small. The verse displays the steering: by the pilot. The figure displays a small rudder governing a great ship. The verse displays the ship: huge. The verse displays the helm: tiny. The verse displays the governor: steering. The figure of the small helm steering the great ship displays the second illustration of the tongue's power: a huge ship, driven by fierce winds, is steered wherever the pilot wills by a very small rudder — so the small tongue directs the whole course of a man. The verse displays the small helm, whose lesson the tongue's great boasting confirms (v.5).
5:
"Even so the tongue is a little member, and boasteth great things. Behold, how great a matter a little fire kindleth!" The witnesses note the tongue a little member boasting great things, like a small fire. On "the tongue is a little member, and boasteth great things," the grammatical voices note the application. Barnes and Robertson note the point of the two figures: "even so the tongue is a little member (a small part of the body), and boasteth great things (makes great claims, achieves or vaunts great effects)" — like the bit and the rudder, the tongue is small but accomplishes (or boasts of) great things, for good or ill.

On "boasteth great things," the witnesses note the disproportion. Barnes and Calvin note that the tongue, small as it is, has vast power and makes great boasts; its influence, like its claims, is out of all proportion to its size.

On "how great a matter a little fire kindleth," the grammatical-historical note the new figure of fire: "how great a matter (forest, or pile of wood) a little fire kindleth (sets ablaze)" — a tiny spark can set a great forest on fire; the figure shifts from control (bit, helm) to destruction (fire), introducing the tongue's power to do immense harm. The verse displays the tongue a little member boasting great things, like a small fire.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the application — "the tongue is a little member, and boasteth great things," the small tongue accomplishing or vaunting great effects, like the bit and rudder (Barnes, Robertson); the tongue's power and boasts out of proportion to its size (Barnes, Calvin); "how great a matter (forest) a little fire kindleth," a tiny spark setting a great forest ablaze, the figure shifting to destruction (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the tongue's power. The verse marks the tongue: little member. The verse displays the boast: great things. The verse displays the figure: a little fire. The verse displays the effect: a great blaze. The teaching displays the small tongue with vast power. The verse displays the member: little. The verse displays the boasting: great. The verse displays the fire: kindling. The tongue a little member boasting great things, like a small fire, displays the turn from the tongue's power to its peril: small as it is, the tongue boasts and achieves great things — and, like a little spark that sets a whole forest ablaze, it can kindle immense destruction. The verse displays the kindling tongue, the very fire of hell (v.6).
6:
"And the tongue is a fire, a world of iniquity: so is the tongue among our members, that it defileth the whole body, and setteth on fire the course of nature; and it is set on fire of hell." The witnesses note the tongue as a fire and a world of iniquity. On "the tongue is a fire, a world of iniquity," the grammatical voices note the dread description. Barnes and Robertson note the figures piled up: "the tongue is a fire (a destructive blaze), a world of iniquity (a whole world of wickedness, the sum and microcosm of evil)" — the tongue is like a destructive fire and contains a world of evil, capable of every kind of sin.

On "it defileth the whole body, and setteth on fire the course of nature," the witnesses note the spreading corruption. Barnes and Calvin note that the tongue "defileth (pollutes, stains) the whole body, and setteth on fire the course of nature (ton trochon tes geneseos — the wheel of nature/birth, the whole circle of life)" — the tongue corrupts the whole person and sets ablaze the entire course of human life; the phrase ("wheel of nature") is difficult but means the whole round of existence.

On "set on fire of hell," the eastern and grammatical-historical note the source: the tongue "is set on fire of hell (gehenna — its evil fire kindled from hell itself)" — the destructive fire of the tongue is ultimately kindled by hell, the source of its corrupting power; the tongue's evil has a diabolical origin. The verse displays the tongue as a fire and a world of iniquity.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the dread description — "the tongue is a fire, a world of iniquity," a destructive blaze and a whole world of wickedness (Barnes, Robertson); "defileth the whole body, and setteth on fire the course of nature (the wheel/round of life)," corrupting the whole person and the entire course of life (Barnes, Calvin); "set on fire of hell (gehenna)," its evil fire kindled from hell, a diabolical origin (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the fiery tongue. The verse marks the fire: the tongue. The verse displays the world: of iniquity. The verse displays the defiling: the whole body. The verse displays the source: hell. The description displays the tongue as a hell-kindled, all-corrupting fire. The verse displays the tongue: a fire. The verse displays the iniquity: a world. The verse displays the hell: its source. The tongue as a fire and a world of iniquity displays the dread power of the tongue for evil: it is a fire and a whole world of wickedness, defiling the entire person and setting ablaze the whole course of life — its destructive fire kindled from hell itself. The verse displays the hell-kindled tongue, untamable by man (vv.7-8).
7:
"For every kind of beasts, and of birds, and of serpents, and of things in the sea, is tamed, and hath been tamed of mankind:" The brief verse notes that every kind of creature has been tamed by man. The grammatical voices note the universal taming. Barnes and Robertson note the sweep of the statement: "every kind (phusis — nature, species) of beasts, and of birds, and of serpents, and of things in the sea, is tamed, and hath been tamed (subdued, domesticated) of (by) mankind" — every class of animal — land beasts, birds, reptiles, sea creatures — has been and is being tamed by human skill.

On "is tamed... of mankind," the witnesses note human mastery. Barnes and Calvin note that man, given dominion over the creatures, has succeeded in taming even the wildest of every kind; human ingenuity subdues the animal world.

On the contrast prepared, the grammatical-historical note the point: this universal taming of animals sets up the startling contrast of v.8 — man tames every beast, yet cannot tame his own tongue; the most masterful creature cannot master his own small member. The verse displays that every kind of creature has been tamed by man.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the universal taming — "every kind of beasts, birds, serpents, and things in the sea, is tamed... of (by) mankind," every class of animal subdued by human skill (Barnes, Robertson); man, given dominion, taming even the wildest of every kind (Barnes, Calvin); the universal taming setting up the contrast that man cannot tame his own tongue (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the taming of creatures. The verse marks the creatures: every kind. The verse displays the classes: beasts, birds, serpents, sea. The verse displays the taming: by man. The verse displays the contrast: the untamable tongue. The statement displays man mastering all the animal world. The verse displays the beasts: tamed. The verse displays the mankind: taming. The verse displays the mastery: universal. That every kind of creature has been tamed by man displays the foil for the tongue's untamability: man has tamed every class of beast, bird, reptile, and sea creature — yet, as the next verse shows, he cannot tame his own tongue. The verse displays the universal taming, against which the tongue stands untamable (v.8).
8:
"But the tongue can no man tame; it is an unruly evil, full of deadly poison." The brief verse notes that no man can tame the tongue. The grammatical voices note the startling contrast. Barnes and Robertson note the climax: "but the tongue can no man tame" — in contrast to all the tamable creatures (v.7), the tongue defies human taming; no one can fully subdue it by his own power; it is the one untamable thing.

On "an unruly evil," the witnesses note its restless wickedness. Barnes and Calvin note that the tongue is "an unruly (akatastaton — restless, unstable, ungovernable) evil" — a restless, uncontrollable source of mischief, never at rest, always liable to break out in evil.

On "full of deadly poison," the grammatical-historical note the venom: the tongue is "full of deadly poison (the venom of a serpent)" — like a serpent's poison, the tongue's words can kill; slander, lies, and cursing are deadly venom; the figure of the most dangerous of the creatures (the serpent) applied to the tongue. The verse displays that no man can tame the tongue.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the contrast — "the tongue can no man tame," the tongue defying human taming, the one untamable thing (Barnes, Robertson); "an unruly (restless, ungovernable) evil," a restless source of mischief never at rest (Barnes, Calvin); "full of deadly poison," like a serpent's venom, words that can kill (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the untamable tongue. The verse marks the taming: by no man. The verse displays the evil: unruly. The verse displays the poison: deadly. The verse displays the venom: serpent-like. The contrast displays the tongue beyond human mastery. The verse displays the tongue: untamed. The verse displays the evil: restless. The verse displays the poison: lethal. That no man can tame the tongue displays the startling climax of the contrast: though man tames every beast, the tongue defies all human taming — a restless, ungovernable evil, full of deadly poison like a serpent's venom. The verse displays the untamable tongue, whose inconsistency blessing and cursing expose (vv.9-10).
9:
"Therewith bless we God, even the Father; and therewith curse we men, which are made after the similitude of God." The witnesses note the inconsistency of blessing God and cursing men with the same tongue. On "therewith bless we God, even the Father," the grammatical voices note the high use. Barnes and Robertson note the noblest function: "therewith (with the tongue) bless we God, even the Father" — the same tongue is used to bless, praise, and worship God the Father; its highest and proper use is the praise of God.

On "therewith curse we men," the witnesses note the base use. Barnes and Calvin note the contradiction: "therewith curse we men" — the very same tongue curses, reviles, and wishes evil to fellow men; the same instrument used for blessing God is used for cursing men.

On "which are made after the similitude of God," the grammatical-historical note the aggravation: men are "made after the similitude (likeness, image) of God" — to curse men is to dishonour the image of God in them; the inconsistency is the worse because the men cursed bear God's likeness; cursing them reflects on their Maker. The verse displays the inconsistency of blessing God and cursing men with the same tongue.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the high use — "therewith bless we God, even the Father," the tongue's noblest function the praise of God (Barnes, Robertson); "therewith curse we men," the same tongue cursing and reviling fellow men, the contradiction (Barnes, Calvin); "made after the similitude of God," cursing men dishonouring God's image in them, aggravating the inconsistency (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the double use. The verse marks the blessing: of God. The verse displays the cursing: of men. The verse displays the contradiction: same tongue. The verse displays the aggravation: God's image cursed. The inconsistency displays the tongue blessing God yet cursing his image. The verse displays the blessing: high. The verse displays the cursing: base. The verse displays the similitude: of God. The inconsistency of blessing God and cursing men with the same tongue displays the tongue's glaring contradiction: with it we bless God the Father, and with it we curse men who are made in God's likeness — dishonouring the Maker in his image. The verse displays the double-tongued contradiction, which ought not so to be (v.10).
10:
"Out of the same mouth proceedeth blessing and cursing. My brethren, these things ought not so to be." The brief verse notes the rebuke that blessing and cursing should not come from one mouth. The grammatical voices note the unnatural contradiction. Barnes and Robertson note the summary of the inconsistency: "out of the same mouth proceedeth blessing and cursing" — from one and the same mouth come both blessing (of God) and cursing (of men); the two utterly opposed streams flow from a single source.

On "out of the same mouth," the witnesses note the single source. Barnes and Calvin note the wrongness of one mouth producing such contradictory things — blessing and cursing; the mouth that praises God should not also curse; the contradiction is unnatural and indefensible.

On "these things ought not so to be," the puritan Henry notes the rebuke: "my brethren, these things ought not so to be" — a plain, earnest rebuke; such inconsistency is wrong and must not continue; the believer's speech should be consistent, all of one kind, befitting one who blesses God. The verse displays the rebuke that blessing and cursing should not come from one mouth.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the contradiction — "out of the same mouth proceedeth blessing and cursing," two opposed streams from one source (Barnes, Robertson); the wrongness of one mouth producing such contradictory things, unnatural and indefensible (Barnes, Calvin); "these things ought not so to be," the plain rebuke, such inconsistency wrong and not to continue (Henry). The verse displays the rebuke. The verse marks the mouth: the same. The verse displays the streams: blessing and cursing. The verse displays the wrongness: contradictory. The verse displays the rebuke: ought not so. The summary displays one mouth wrongly yielding blessing and cursing. The verse displays the blessing: and cursing. The verse displays the mouth: single. The verse displays the rebuke: plain. The rebuke that blessing and cursing should not come from one mouth displays the plain verdict on the tongue's inconsistency: from the same mouth come both blessing and cursing — and, the apostle says earnestly, these things ought not so to be. The verse displays the rebuked contradiction, shown unnatural by spring and tree (vv.11-12).
11:
"Doth a fountain send forth at the same place sweet water and bitter?" The brief verse notes the figure of the fountain that cannot yield both sweet and bitter. The grammatical voices note the natural impossibility. Barnes and Robertson note the rhetorical question from nature: "doth a fountain send forth at the same place (opening, hole) sweet (fresh) water and bitter (brackish, salt)?" — a single spring does not pour out both fresh and bitter water from the same opening; nature does not produce such contradiction.

On "sweet water and bitter," the witnesses note the two waters. Barnes and Calvin note that a fountain yields one kind of water, not two opposite kinds; sweet and bitter do not flow from one source; the figure exposes the unnaturalness of the tongue's double output.

On the application, the grammatical-historical note the point: as a fountain cannot give both sweet and bitter, so the mouth ought not to give both blessing and cursing (v.10); the consistency of nature rebukes the inconsistency of the tongue. The verse displays the figure of the fountain that cannot yield both sweet and bitter.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the question from nature — "doth a fountain send forth at the same place (opening) sweet (fresh) water and bitter?" a single spring not pouring out both fresh and bitter (Barnes, Robertson); a fountain yielding one kind of water, not two opposite kinds (Barnes, Calvin); the natural consistency rebuking the tongue's double output (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the fountain. The verse marks the fountain: one source. The verse displays the waters: sweet and bitter. The verse displays the impossibility: not both. The verse displays the application: the inconsistent tongue. The figure displays nature yielding one kind, not contradictory streams. The verse displays the fountain: single. The verse displays the sweet: and bitter. The verse displays the rebuke: from nature. The figure of the fountain that cannot yield both sweet and bitter displays nature's rebuke of the tongue: a spring does not send forth both fresh and bitter water from the same opening — so the mouth ought not to pour out both blessing and cursing. The verse displays the consistent fountain, reinforced by the fig tree and vine (v.12).
12:
"Can the fig tree, my brethren, bear olive berries? either a vine, figs? so can no fountain both yield salt water and fresh." The witnesses note the figures of tree and vine: each yields after its kind. On "can the fig tree bear olive berries? either a vine, figs?" the grammatical voices note the law of kind. Barnes and Robertson note the further illustrations from nature: "can the fig tree bear olive berries? either a vine, figs?" — each tree bears its own proper fruit; a fig tree cannot bear olives, nor a vine figs; nature holds each plant to its own kind.

On "so can no fountain both yield salt water and fresh," the witnesses note the return to the spring. Barnes and Calvin note the conclusion: "so can no fountain both yield salt water and fresh" — just as no tree bears alien fruit, no spring yields both salt and fresh water; the source determines the output, consistently after its kind.

On the application, the puritan Henry notes the lesson: as nature is consistent — each source yielding only its own kind — so the heart and tongue should be consistent; a heart truly devoted to God will not pour out cursing; the inconsistency of blessing and cursing betrays a divided or unrenewed heart. The verse displays the figures of tree and vine: each yields after its kind.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the law of kind — "can the fig tree bear olive berries? either a vine, figs?" each tree bearing its own proper fruit, not alien fruit (Barnes, Robertson); "no fountain both yield salt water and fresh," no spring yielding both, the source determining the output (Barnes, Calvin); the consistency of nature teaching that heart and tongue should be consistent, the double tongue betraying a divided heart (Henry). The verse displays the law of kind. The verse marks the fig tree: not olives. The verse displays the vine: not figs. The verse displays the fountain: not both. The verse displays the lesson: consistency. The figures display each source yielding only its own kind. The verse displays the tree: after its kind. The verse displays the vine: after its kind. The verse displays the spring: consistent. The figures of tree and vine — each yields after its kind — display the law of nature that rebukes the tongue: a fig tree cannot bear olives, nor a vine figs, nor a spring both salt and fresh water; so the heart truly given to God should not pour out both blessing and cursing. The verse displays the consistency of nature, calling for the meekness of true wisdom (v.13).
13:
"Who is a wise man and endued with knowledge among you? let him shew out of a good conversation his works with meekness of wisdom." The witnesses note that true wisdom is shown by a good life with meekness. On "who is a wise man and endued with knowledge among you?" the grammatical voices note the challenge. Barnes and Robertson note the question opening the theme of wisdom: "who is a wise man (sophos) and endued with knowledge (epistemon — understanding, skilled) among you?" — James asks who claims to be truly wise and understanding; the question tests the nature of real wisdom.

On "let him shew out of a good conversation his works," the witnesses note the proof in conduct. Barnes and Calvin note the answer: "let him shew (demonstrate) out of a good conversation (kales anastrophes — good conduct, manner of life) his works" — true wisdom is proved not by words or claims but by a good life and good deeds; as faith is shown by works (ch. 2), so wisdom is shown by conduct.

On "with meekness of wisdom," the puritan Henry notes the manner: the works are to be shown "with meekness of wisdom (the gentleness that belongs to true wisdom)" — genuine wisdom is humble and gentle, not proud or contentious; meekness is its hallmark, distinguishing it from the bitter, self-assertive false wisdom (vv.14-16). The verse displays that true wisdom is shown by a good life with meekness.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the challenge — "who is a wise man and endued with knowledge (understanding) among you?" testing who is truly wise (Barnes, Robertson); "let him shew out of a good conversation (conduct) his works," wisdom proved by a good life and deeds, not words (Barnes, Calvin); "with meekness of wisdom," genuine wisdom humble and gentle, meekness its hallmark (Henry). The verse displays true wisdom. The verse marks the question: who is wise. The verse displays the proof: a good life. The verse displays the works: shown. The verse displays the manner: meekness of wisdom. The challenge displays wisdom proved by gentle, good conduct. The verse displays the wise: tested. The verse displays the conversation: good. The verse displays the meekness: of wisdom. That true wisdom is shown by a good life with meekness displays the test of real wisdom: whoever claims to be wise and understanding must prove it not by words but by a good life and good works, done in the meekness that belongs to true wisdom. The verse displays the meekness of wisdom, over against bitter envying and strife (v.14).
14:
"But if ye have bitter envying and strife in your hearts, glory not, and lie not against the truth." The witnesses note the warning against bitter envy and strife masquerading as wisdom. On "if ye have bitter envying and strife in your hearts," the grammatical voices note the false wisdom's marks. Barnes and Robertson note the contrast with meek wisdom: "if ye have bitter envying (zelon pikron — bitter zeal, jealousy) and strife (eritheian — selfish ambition, rivalry, factiousness) in your hearts" — the marks of false wisdom are bitter jealousy and self-seeking contention harboured in the heart.

On "glory not," the witnesses note the forbidden boast. Barnes and Calvin note: "glory not (do not boast)" — those with such bitter, contentious hearts are not to boast of being wise; their inward envy and strife belie any claim to wisdom; boasting of wisdom while harbouring such things is empty.

On "lie not against the truth," the grammatical-historical note the falsehood: to claim wisdom while full of bitter envy is to "lie against the truth" — to contradict the reality; such a claim is a lie, for true wisdom and bitter strife cannot coexist; to boast of wisdom amid envy is to falsify the truth. The verse displays the warning against bitter envy and strife masquerading as wisdom.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the false marks — "if ye have bitter envying (jealousy) and strife (selfish ambition) in your hearts," bitter jealousy and contention the marks of false wisdom (Barnes, Robertson); "glory not," not to boast of wisdom while harbouring such things, the boast empty (Barnes, Calvin); "lie not against the truth," claiming wisdom amid envy a lie contradicting reality (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the false wisdom. The verse marks the envy: bitter. The verse displays the strife: selfish. The verse displays the boast: forbidden. The verse displays the lie: against truth. The warning displays envy and strife belying any claim to wisdom. The verse displays the envying: bitter. The verse displays the strife: in the heart. The verse displays the truth: not to be lied against. The warning against bitter envy and strife masquerading as wisdom displays the test that exposes false wisdom: those who harbour bitter jealousy and selfish strife in their hearts must not boast of being wise, nor lie against the truth — for such a claim contradicts reality. The verse displays the false wisdom, which is earthly, sensual, devilish (v.15).
15:
"This wisdom descendeth not from above, but is earthly, sensual, devilish." The brief verse notes the threefold character of false wisdom: earthly, sensual, devilish. The grammatical voices note its origin and nature. Barnes and Robertson note the verdict on the false wisdom: "this wisdom descendeth not from above (is not from God, not heavenly), but is earthly (epigeios — belonging to the earth, worldly), sensual (psychike — natural, animal, unspiritual), devilish (daimoniodes — demon-like, from demons)" — the wisdom marked by bitter envy and strife does not come from God but is of a threefold base origin.

On "earthly... sensual," the witnesses note the lower nature. Barnes and Calvin note that this wisdom is "earthly" (concerned only with the things of this world) and "sensual" (springing from the unrenewed natural man, the animal soul, not the Spirit) — a wisdom of the flesh and the world, not of heaven.

On "devilish," the grammatical-historical note the dark source: "devilish (demon-like)" — its character is like that of the demons, sharing their envy and strife; the false wisdom descends from below, partaking of the very nature of evil spirits. The verse displays the threefold character of false wisdom: earthly, sensual, devilish.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the verdict — "this wisdom descendeth not from above (not from God), but is earthly (worldly), sensual (natural, unspiritual), devilish (demon-like)," the false wisdom of a threefold base origin (Barnes, Robertson); "earthly... sensual," concerned with this world and springing from the unrenewed natural man (Barnes, Calvin); "devilish," like the demons in envy and strife, from below (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the false wisdom's nature. The verse marks the origin: not from above. The verse displays the earthly: worldly. The verse displays the sensual: natural. The verse displays the devilish: demon-like. The verdict displays the false wisdom of earth, flesh, and hell. The verse displays the wisdom: not heavenly. The verse displays the sensual: unspiritual. The verse displays the devilish: from demons. The threefold character of false wisdom — earthly, sensual, devilish — displays the dark verdict on the wisdom of envy and strife: it does not come down from God but is worldly in its sphere, natural and unspiritual in its source, and demon-like in its character. The verse displays the false wisdom, whose fruit is confusion and every evil work (v.16).
16:
"For where envying and strife is, there is confusion and every evil work." The brief verse notes that envy and strife breed confusion and every evil. The grammatical voices note the fruit of false wisdom. Barnes and Robertson note the consequence: "where envying (jealousy) and strife (selfish ambition, faction) is, there is confusion (akatastasia — disorder, instability, tumult) and every evil work (every kind of base deed)" — wherever bitter jealousy and contention reign, the result is disorder and all manner of evil.

On "confusion," the witnesses note the disorder. Barnes and Calvin note that "confusion" is the social disorder, instability, and tumult that envy and strife produce — division, unrest, and chaos in the community; the opposite of the peace that marks heavenly wisdom.

On "every evil work," the grammatical-historical note the breadth: "every evil work (every base deed)" — envy and strife open the door to all kinds of wickedness; once they reign, no evil is excluded; the fruit of false wisdom is comprehensive corruption. The verse displays that envy and strife breed confusion and every evil.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the consequence — "where envying (jealousy) and strife (faction) is, there is confusion (disorder, tumult) and every evil work," jealousy and contention producing disorder and all manner of evil (Barnes, Robertson); "confusion" the social disorder and instability they produce, the opposite of peace (Barnes, Calvin); "every evil work," envy and strife opening the door to all wickedness (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the fruit of false wisdom. The verse marks the envy: and strife. The verse displays the result: confusion. The verse displays the disorder: tumult. The verse displays the evil: every kind. The consequence displays envy and strife breeding chaos and evil. The verse displays the envying: present. The verse displays the confusion: produced. The verse displays the evil: comprehensive. That envy and strife breed confusion and every evil displays the bitter fruit of the false wisdom: wherever jealousy and selfish contention reign, the result is disorder and every kind of base deed — comprehensive corruption, the opposite of heaven's peace. The verse displays the fruit of strife, against the pure wisdom from above (v.17).
17:
"But the wisdom that is from above is first pure, then peaceable, gentle, and easy to be intreated, full of mercy and good fruits, without partiality, and without hypocrisy." The witnesses note the sevenfold description of the wisdom from above. On "the wisdom that is from above is first pure," the grammatical voices note its primary mark. Barnes and Robertson note the catalogue of heavenly wisdom's qualities: "the wisdom that is from above (from God, heavenly) is first pure (hagne — chaste, holy, free from defilement)" — true wisdom is first of all pure; purity is its foremost and fundamental quality, placed first deliberately.

On "then peaceable, gentle, and easy to be intreated," the witnesses note its social graces. Barnes and Calvin note the next qualities: "peaceable (peace-loving, not contentious), gentle (forbearing, considerate, reasonable), and easy to be intreated (eupeithes — willing to yield, compliant, open to persuasion)" — heavenly wisdom makes peace, is gentle and forbearing, and is reasonable and ready to yield; the opposite of the strife of false wisdom.

On "full of mercy and good fruits, without partiality, and without hypocrisy," the puritan Henry notes the remaining marks: "full of mercy and good fruits (compassionate and fruitful in good deeds), without partiality (impartial, undivided, not wavering), and without hypocrisy (sincere, genuine)" — heavenly wisdom is merciful and active in good, impartial and sincere; the complete portrait of the wisdom from God. The verse displays the sevenfold description of the wisdom from above.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the catalogue — "the wisdom from above is first pure (holy, chaste)," purity its foremost mark, placed first (Barnes, Robertson); "peaceable, gentle, and easy to be intreated," peace-loving, forbearing, reasonable, ready to yield (Barnes, Calvin); "full of mercy and good fruits, without partiality, and without hypocrisy," merciful, fruitful, impartial, sincere (Henry). The verse displays heavenly wisdom. The verse marks the wisdom: from above. The verse displays the first mark: pure. The verse displays the graces: peaceable, gentle, yielding. The verse displays the fruits: mercy, sincerity. The description displays the full portrait of God's wisdom. The verse displays the purity: first. The verse displays the peace: loving. The verse displays the mercy: full. The sevenfold description of the wisdom from above displays the beautiful portrait of true wisdom: it is first pure, then peaceable, gentle, and ready to yield, full of mercy and good fruits, impartial and sincere — every quality the opposite of the bitter, contentious false wisdom. The verse displays the wisdom from above, which sows the fruit of righteousness in peace (v.18).
18:
"And the fruit of righteousness is sown in peace of them that make peace." The brief verse notes that the fruit of righteousness is sown in peace by peacemakers. The grammatical voices note the closing principle. Barnes and Robertson note the harvest of heavenly wisdom: "the fruit of righteousness (the fruit which is righteousness, or which righteousness yields) is sown in peace of (by) them that make peace (the peacemakers)" — the harvest of righteousness comes from seed sown in peace by those who are peaceable; peace is the soil and the manner in which righteousness grows.

On "the fruit of righteousness... sown in peace," the witnesses note the connection. Barnes and Calvin note that righteousness is produced not amid strife but in peace; the peaceable disposition (the mark of heavenly wisdom, v.17) is the condition under which the fruit of righteousness is sown and grows; strife yields confusion (v.16), but peace yields righteousness.

On "them that make peace," the puritan Henry notes the peacemakers: "them that make peace" — those who promote peace; the peacemakers are the sowers, and the harvest of righteousness is theirs; the chapter closes by commending the peaceable spirit as the soil of all good fruit. The verse displays that the fruit of righteousness is sown in peace by peacemakers.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the harvest — "the fruit of righteousness is sown in peace of (by) them that make peace," the harvest of righteousness from seed sown in peace by the peaceable (Barnes, Robertson); righteousness produced in peace not strife, the peaceable disposition the condition of its growth (Barnes, Calvin); "them that make peace," the peacemakers the sowers whose harvest is righteousness (Henry). The verse displays the fruit of peace. The verse marks the fruit: of righteousness. The verse displays the sowing: in peace. The verse displays the sowers: peacemakers. The verse displays the harvest: righteousness. The principle displays righteousness growing in the soil of peace. The verse displays the righteousness: sown. The verse displays the peace: the soil. The verse displays the peacemakers: rewarded. That the fruit of righteousness is sown in peace by peacemakers displays the closing principle of the chapter on wisdom: the harvest of righteousness springs from seed sown in peace by those who make peace — for the peaceable spirit of heavenly wisdom is the soil in which all good fruit grows. The verse displays the harvest of peace, closing the chapter on the tongue and the two kinds of wisdom.
